1.title: Camera Iris
1.body_md:
**Your eyes already do this.** Squinting makes distant things a little sharper — but also darker. The Camera Iris works the same way: wide open lets in more light but blurs the background; very closed keeps everything in focus but needs more light.

The side effect of a wide-open Camera Iris can be beautiful: background lights turn into soft glowing circles called *bokeh*.

frames.label: Wide open — shallow depth of field, strong bokeh
Very closed — deep depth of field

1.metaphor_line: The sensor is a bucket — the Camera Iris is how wide the mouth opens.
1.scene_caption: The scene is a Blender render: Suzanne — a classic 3D test mesh — surrounded by orbiting cubes that emit soft glowing light. Opening the iris wider turns those cube lights into the round blur called *bokeh*.

2.title: Capture Duration
2.subtitle: But what if it's still too bright — or too dark?
2.body_pre: The Camera Iris controls how *wide* the light stream is. The **Capture Duration** controls how *long* the sensor collects light.
2.body_post:
Sports photographers use a short Capture Duration to freeze action. Night photographers use a long one to gather light trails.

A long Capture Duration makes moving subjects **streak** across the frame — we call this *motion streaking* in this lesson.

3.title: Capture Duration — try it
3.subtitle: Drag the slider to see how the Capture Duration affects motion.
3.metaphor_line: The Capture Duration is how long the bucket sits in the rain.

4.title: Sensor Sensitivity
4.subtitle: You've opened the Camera Iris wide. The Capture Duration is as long as you can hold steady. It's still too dark.
4.body_pre: You've run out of light. The last option: make the sensor **more sensitive to light**. It amplifies the signal — but also amplifies noise.
4.body_post: Sensitivity is the last resort: a noisy photo is better than a completely dark one. You can always reduce noise in editing — you can't add light that wasn't captured.

5.title: Sensor Sensitivity — try it
5.subtitle: Toggle between low, medium, and high to see the noise tradeoff.

frames.label: Low sensitivity — clean, needs lots of light

5.metaphor_line: Sensitivity is how big the bucket is — bigger catches more, but also more debris (noise).

6.title: Sensor Sensitivity — A / B compare
6.subtitle: Drag the divider to slide between low and high sensitivity. Low is clean but needs light; high fills in dark scenes at the cost of grain.

7.title: The Exposure Triangle
7.subtitle: How the three settings share one light budget
7.body: Every change is a tradeoff. Brightening with one setting lets you darken with another — and each has a side effect.
7.insight: The art of photography is choosing which tradeoff serves your image best.
